This proposal takes a conservative approach to reducing price manipulation risk for thinly traded assets in the aftermath of the Mango hack, at the cost of protocol growth. In conjunction with this proposal, the community should find ways to encourage migration to Compound III which is safer from a risk management perspective.
Thank you Chainlink, Getty, and the community for developing this proposal; it adds robustness to the price feed while preserving the existing structure and fall-back mechanisms.
Closing deprecated markets reduces the surface area of risk for the protocol; gas savings to claim COMP is an easy win for all users.
Having a dedicated price feed coordinator will accelerate the addition of new assets and security; Getty has proven himself capable through his prior work
Lowering the proposal threshold will benefit CAPs and community-led governance efforts

The updated price feed supports four new assets, and has been confirmed to function correctly; technical risk from this proposal is low. Adding MKR as a borrowable asset is also low risk, and prepares the protocol for new functionality.
Adding AAVE, SUSHI, and YFI as borrowable assets is also low risk, and prepares the protocol for new functionality.
These collateral factors are conservative first steps to onboarding the assets, and improve the protocol with little downside.

The Pause Guardian is emergency functionality that can be used to suspend new usage in a market; multiple collateral assets (MKR, AAVE) have their own external governance processes, and the community should be equipped to manage the risk of token changes without relying on Compound Labs.
This proposal acknowledged the concerns laid out in Proposal 32, and responsibly allocates protocol reserves to compensate users
The proposal whitelist will accelerate community development, with few risks/downsides; bravo!

Having a dedicated, professional team focused on managing protocol parameters is a direction that will serve the protocol & community well. I have concerns about the significant size of the COMP grant, but know that the community can revisit this at any time.

Proposal 63 provides two days of optionality to the community to fully evaluate Proposal 64, and can be cancelled prior to execution. This is a useful proposal if an issue is discovered with Proposal 64, or if COMP needs to be disabled prior to 64's execution.
Proposal 64 returns the COMP Distribution to a mostly-functional state. Certain users (and new users) will still be prevented from claiming COMP, which requires a follow-up proposal to fix.
These are modest, well-reasoned updates by Gauntlet, and will slightly increase the competitiveness of the protocol without adding undue risk.
This proposal continues to improve the utility of collateral assets, in a measured way.
